ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Fluid Compartments and Fluid Shifts
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Fluid Compartments and Fluid Shifts | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Fluid Compartments and Fluid Shifts — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Fluid Compartments and Fluid Shifts** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

Canadian items may show **SI units** (mmol/L) for electrolytes and glucose where the stem uses metric labs; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly framing. Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Fluid Compartments and Fluid Shifts** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Items tie **ICF vs ECF**, **osmotic vs hydrostatic forces**, and **where fluid actually is**—choose **perfusion-focused** actions when **intravascular depletion** exists even if **total body water** is high.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **TBW**: ICF vs ECF; sodium drives ECF osmolality and water movement.
- **Shift cues**: Hct/BUN trends, albumin, third-spacing vs true overload.
- **Nursing**: correlate **weight + I&O + vitals** before blaming “too much fluid” alone.
- **Safety**: isotonic boluses expand vascular space more predictably than D5W for resuscitation themes.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **burns client with rising hematocrit and falling BP despite visible edema** brings fluid and electrolyte risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **ECG + K⁺**, **neuro + Na⁺**, **lungs + weight + I&O**, and **ABG trend** before comfort-only tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the Fluids & Electrolytes topic hub (**`fluids-electrolytes`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Fluid balance acute care](LESSON:fluid-balance-acute-care) · [Na disorders](LESSON:hyponatremia-vs-hypernatremia-hy) · [K disorders](LESSON:hypo-vs-hyperkalemia-hy) · [ABG basics](LESSON:abg-interpretation-basics-hy) · [SIADH vs DI basics](LESSON:siadh-vs-di-basics) · [DKA vs HHS](LESSON:dka-vs-hhs-priorities-hy) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Third Spacing and Edema** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Third-spacing** can present as **hypovolemia** with **peripheral edema or ascites**—boards punish choosing **diuretics first** when the stem shows **shock from trapped ECF**; prioritize **volume per orders** and **monitoring**.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Third space**: peritoneum, bowel wall, burns interstitium—fluid not in usable vascular space.
- **Edema**: pitting vs nonpitting; JVD and crackles suggest vascular overload patterns when paired.
- **Assessment**: orthostasis, UO, lactate when sepsis complicates.
- **Teaching**: explain why “swollen but hypotensive” can still need ordered fluids.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Intake**: PO, IV, flushes, medications diluents, enteral feeds.
- **Output**: urine, emesis, drains, stools, insensible estimates when policy requires.
- **Shift totals**: handoff reconciliation errors are safety events.
- **Escalate**: oliguria + rising creatinine or hypotension triggers provider communication.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

**1 kg ≈ 1 L fluid** gain themes—choose **daily same-time weights**, **lung reassessment**, and **provider notification** before increasing home salt when overload is suspected.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Technique**: same scale, void first, minimal clothing when protocol allows.
- **Trend**: small daily gains matter in heart failure pathways.
- **Pair**: JVP, edema, SpO₂, orthopnea with weight jumps.
- **Teaching**: low-sodium diet, fluid restriction literacy, when to call clinic.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.body:
Examiners use **Isotonic, Hypotonic, and Hypertonic IV Fluids** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Distinguish **tonicity in bag vs physiologic effect** (D5W becomes hypotonic after glucose metabolism)—choose **isotonic crystalloids** for acute **hypovolemia** unless stem specifies contraindication.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Isotonic NS/LR**: ECF expansion; watch chloride load with huge NS.
- **Hypotonic 0.45% NaCl**: cellular hydration themes—risk cellular swelling if given too fast in vulnerable clients.
- **Hypertonic 3% NaCl**: severe symptomatic hyponatremia—central line + ICU-style monitoring when item states policy.
- **D5W**: not primary resuscitation fluid; free-water contribution after metabolism.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**IV Fluid Selection for Common Conditions** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

Canadian items may show **SI units** (mmol/L) for electrolytes and glucose where the stem uses metric labs; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly framing. Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **IV Fluid Selection for Common Conditions** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Match **clinical syndrome** to **fluid choice**: **LR vs NS** debates appear as “pick safest ordered option”; **liver failure** may contraindicate lactate metabolism themes when stem lists acute failure.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Sepsis/burns/surgery**: balanced crystalloid themes when item favors LR.
- **Hyponatremia**: avoid hypotonic free water overload in inappropriate ADH states.
- **Hypernatremia**: free water deficit replacement per orders—track rate.
- **Renal failure**: watch potassium in LR when oliguric and hyperkalemic.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **DKA resuscitation with concern for hyperchloremic acidosis from large-volume NS** brings fluid and electrolyte risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **ECG + K⁺**, **neuro + Na⁺**, **lungs + weight + I&O**, and **ABG trend** before comfort-only tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the Fluids & Electrolytes topic hub (**`fluids-electrolytes`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Fluid balance acute care](LESSON:fluid-balance-acute-care) · [Na disorders](LESSON:hyponatremia-vs-hypernatremia-hy) · [K disorders](LESSON:hypo-vs-hyperkalemia-hy) · [ABG basics](LESSON:abg-interpretation-basics-hy) · [SIADH vs DI basics](LESSON:siadh-vs-di-basics) · [DKA vs HHS](LESSON:dka-vs-hhs-priorities-hy) · [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly framing. Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Diabetes Insipidus Deep Dive** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**DI** → **large dilute urine output** and **hypernatremia** risk—choose **replace water deficit**, **DDAVP** per orders for central DI, and **monitor electrolytes** over restricting fluids incorrectly.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Central vs nephrogenic**: DDAVP response themes when item tests differentiation.
- **Monitor**: strict I&O, hourly weights in acute polyuria protocols when ordered.
- **Safety**: rapid sodium correction also injures—track neuro status.
- **Teaching**: thirst access, medication timing, when to seek urgent care.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **post-pituitary surgery with polyuria, dilute urine, and rising serum sodium** brings fluid and electrolyte risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **ECG + K⁺**, **neuro + Na⁺**, **lungs + weight + I&O**, and **ABG trend** before comfort-only tasks when risk is rising.

- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Hypocalcemia and Tetany** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Link **Chvostek/Trousseau**, **tetany**, **seizures**, and **QT prolongation**—choose **airway**, **IV calcium gluconate per orders**, and **monitor** over reassurance alone.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Causes**: surgical hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, massive transfusion, alkalosis.
- **Replace**: oral calcium/vitamin D long-term; acute IV per protocol.
- **Safety**: verify product and line compatibility; avoid extravasation with calcium.
- **Related**: magnesium deficiency worsens tetany—check Mg when refractory.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:

You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways

**Never IV push KCl**; **peripheral max concentration/rate** themes; **cardiac monitoring** for moderate/severe hypokalemia—choose **clarify order** or **use pump + policy limits** over hanging wide open.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Pump mandatory** for concentrated infusions when policy states.
- **Assess**: pain at site, signs of extravasation, ECG when ordered.
- **Never**: add K to hanging bag at bedside unless protocolized and double-checked.
- **Documentation**: infusion start times and nurse double-check signatures when required.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **physician order for 40 mEq KCl in 100 mL NS to run over 20 minutes via peripheral IV** brings fluid and electrolyte risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **ECG + K⁺**, **neuro + Na⁺**, **lungs + weight + I&O**, and **ABG trend** before comfort-only tasks when risk is rising.

- **Bicarbonate**: reserved for severe extremes per orders—not routine for all acidosis.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Acute vs chronic**: chronic may have metabolic compensation (elevated HCO₃).
- **Causes**: opioid excess, airway obstruction, neuromuscular failure, severe pneumonia.
- **Nursing**: RR pattern, accessory muscle use, mentation, ABG trends.
- **Safety**: naloxone when opioid-induced; suction readiness.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

ca-rn-nclex-rn.title: Which Electrolyte Patient Is Unstable?
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Which Electrolyte Patient Is Unstable? | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
ca-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Which Electrolyte Patient Is Unstable? — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Which Electrolyte Patient Is Unstable?** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

Canadian items may show **SI units** (mmol/L) for electrolytes and glucose where the stem uses metric labs; prioritization logic matches NCLEX-RN.

**Pathway context (RN, Canada).** This lesson supports NCLEX-RN preparation with Canada-friendly framing. Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/canada/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Which Electrolyte Patient Is Unstable?** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Pick **hyperkalemia with ECG changes** first—**membrane stabilization + escalation** beats education tasks on stable clients.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Unstable**: arrhythmia, seizure, airway compromise, shock, acute pulmonary edema.
- **Stable**: chronic labs without acute threat—education appropriate later.
- **Triage**: use objective monitoring data over subjective comfort alone.
- **Communication**: SBAR for critical labs.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

**Nursing priorities (high yield)**

- **Teaching**: explain why “swollen but hypotensive” can still need ordered fluids.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.

- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Intake and Output Calculation
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Intake and Output Calculation | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Intake and Output Calculation — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Intake and Output Calculation** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **K⁺ and ECG emergencies**, **safe sodium correction**, **fluid tonicity mistakes**, **ABG interpretation with compensation**, and **DKA/HHS potassium safety**.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Intake and Output Calculation** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

NCLEX tests **ice chips ~ half volume**, **continuous losses** (NG, drains), and **hourly UO** thresholds—choose **accurate documentation** and **early reporting** of **<0.5 mL/kg/hr** over guessing.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Intake**: PO, IV, flushes, medications diluents, enteral feeds.
- **Output**: urine, emesis, drains, stools, insensible estimates when policy requires.
- **Shift totals**: handoff reconciliation errors are safety events.
- **Escalate**: oliguria + rising creatinine or hypotension triggers provider communication.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**

- **Hypotonic 0.45% NaCl**: cellular hydration themes—risk cellular swelling if given too fast in vulnerable clients.
- **Hypertonic 3% NaCl**: severe symptomatic hyponatremia—central line + ICU-style monitoring when item states policy.
- **D5W**: not primary resuscitation fluid; free-water contribution after metabolism.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Dehydration vs Hypovolemia
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Dehydration vs Hypovolemia | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Dehydration vs Hypovolemia — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Dehydration vs Hypovolemia** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **K⁺ and ECG emergencies**, **safe sodium correction**, **fluid tonicity mistakes**, **ABG interpretation with compensation**, and **DKA/HHS potassium safety**.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Dehydration vs Hypovolemia** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Dehydration** can exist with **stable BP** early; **hypovolemic shock** adds **perfusion failure**—choose **escalation** when **tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria, lactate** cluster, not teaching alone.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Signs**: turgor limits in older adults; axillary moisture; orthostasis.
- **Causes**: GI losses, fever, diuretics, diabetes insipidus losses.
- **Interventions**: oral rehydration when safe swallow; IV when unable or unstable.
- **Labs**: BUN:Cr pre-renal pattern when stem supports.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Hypokalemia Nursing Priorities
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Hypokalemia Nursing Priorities | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Hypokalemia Nursing Priorities — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Hypokalemia Nursing Priorities** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **K⁺ and ECG emergencies**, **safe sodium correction**, **fluid tonicity mistakes**, **ABG interpretation with compensation**, and **DKA/HHS potassium safety**.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Hypokalemia Nursing Priorities** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Prioritize **never IV push potassium**, **oral replacement when safe**, **never exceed max IV rate/concentration** per facility policy, and **replete magnesium** when refractory—choose **continuous monitoring** for **<3.0** patterns.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Manifestations**: weakness, ileus, arrhythmia risk with digitalis.
- **Replacement**: PO preferred when GI functional; IV requires pump and frequent rechecks.
- **Cause**: diuretics, GI losses, alkalosis—treat underlying driver when orders exist.
- **Safety**: renal-adjust oral/IV dosing themes when stem lists CKD.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Hypomagnesemia and Hypermagnesemia
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Hypo- and Hypermagnesemia | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Hypo- and Hypermagnesemia — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Hypomagnesemia and Hypermagnesemia** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **K⁺ and ECG emergencies**, **safe sodium correction**, **fluid tonicity mistakes**, **ABG interpretation with compensation**, and **DKA/HHS potassium safety**.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Hypomagnesemia and Hypermagnesemia** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**Hypomagnesemia** potentiates **hypokalemia** and **torsades**—choose **replace magnesium first** when stem pairs both; **hypermagnesemia** → **weakness**, **hypoventilation**, **loss of reflexes**, **cardiac arrest**—choose **calcium antagonism per orders**, **airway**, **dialysis** themes.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Low Mg**: tremor, cramps, arrhythmias; alcoholism, diuretics, diarrhea.
- **High Mg**: flush, hypotension, respiratory depression—watch post-MgSO₄ OB clients too.
- **Replace**: oral vs IV magnesium sulfate per protocol; deep tendon reflex checks when on infusion.
- **Safety**: renal failure raises toxicity risk—slow rates and monitoring.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **refactory hypokalemia on telemetry with torsades after diuretic use** brings fluid and electrolyte risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **ECG + K⁺**, **neuro + Na⁺**, **lungs + weight + I&O**, and **ABG trend** before comfort-only tasks when risk is rising.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: Sodium Correction Safety
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: Sodium Correction Safety | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: Sodium Correction Safety — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**Sodium Correction Safety** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **K⁺ and ECG emergencies**, **safe sodium correction**, **fluid tonicity mistakes**, **ABG interpretation with compensation**, and **DKA/HHS potassium safety**.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Sodium Correction Safety** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**ODS (CPM)** risk with **too-fast correction**—choose **stop hypertonic bolus**, **notify provider**, **monitor neuro**, and **DDAVP rescue** themes when item describes overcorrection.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Targets**: follow institutional limits; chronic vs acute timelines matter.
- **Monitor**: neuro checks during every sodium-moving therapy.
- **Free water**: may be used to relower sodium when iatrogenic overcorrection occurs per protocol.
- **Documentation**: hourly totals of hypertonic saline given.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **chronic hyponatremia correcting 18 mEq in 12 hours with new dysarthria** brings fluid and electrolyte risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **ECG + K⁺**, **neuro + Na⁺**, **lungs + weight + I&O**, and **ABG trend** before comfort-only tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the Fluids & Electrolytes topic hub (**`fluids-electrolytes`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.

- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- **Safety**: naloxone when opioid-induced; suction readiness.

- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

us-rn-nclex-rn.title: ABG Compensation Step-by-Step
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoTitle: ABG Compensation Step-by-Step | NCLEX-RN | NurseNest
us-rn-nclex-rn.seoDescription: Clinical review: ABG Compensation Step-by-Step — Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base. Board-style clinical judgment: priorities, red flags, and first-line nursing actions.

sections.heading: Clinical meaning
sections.body:
**ABG Compensation Step-by-Step** (Fluids, Electrolytes & Acid-Base) links **volume status, tonicity, electrolyte shifts, and acid–base balance** to nursing judgment: recognize **hyperkalemia with conduction emergencies**, **symptomatic hyponatremia and overcorrection risk**, **pulmonary edema from fluid overload**, **unsafe potassium infusions**, **calcium/tetany emergencies**, **magnesium toxicity**, and **ABG patterns** under pressure—and escalate when airway, perfusion, or rhythm is threatened.

US NCLEX-RN items often test **K⁺ and ECG emergencies**, **safe sodium correction**, **fluid tonicity mistakes**, **ABG interpretation with compensation**, and **DKA/HHS potassium safety**.

**Pathway context (RN, United States).** Continue with related lessons from the [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons).

**Learning objectives**
- Integrate **vitals, I&O, weights, lung exam, neuro status, ECG, and BMP/ABG** to identify fluid and electrolyte emergencies.
- Select **nursing interventions and teaching** aligned with orders, scope, pharmacy protocols, and facility policy.
- Communicate **early** when findings suggest **unstable hyperkalemia**, **seizing hyponatremia**, **respiratory failure with acidosis**, **rapid sodium shifts**, or **incompatible IV electrolyte orders**.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **ABG Compensation Step-by-Step** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

**ROME**: respiratory opposite; metabolic equal—then check if compensation is **appropriate** or **mixed**—choose the interpretation that matches **dominant disorder** before picking an intervention.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Steps**: pH → primary component → expected compensation range → PaO₂ last for oxygenation plan.
- **Mixed**: when compensation overshoots or second disorder present.
- **O₂**: never ignore hypoxemia while fixing acid-base.
- **Context**: FiO₂, baseline CO₂ retainer status changes targets.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

sections.heading: Clinical scenario
sections.body:
**Patient vignette.** A client scenario related to **ABG with pH 7.32, PaCO₂ 55, HCO₃ 29** brings fluid and electrolyte risks to the foreground. You pair **focused assessment** (vitals, lung sounds, edema, neuro checks, ECG, strict I&O) with **labs** when provided, follow **IV and electrolyte orders with double-checks**, and **notify the provider or activate the emergency team** when policy criteria are met.

**Clinical application:** The correct "first" action closes the **highest-risk gap**—usually **airway/oxygen**, **ECG/telemetry escalation**, **stop harmful infusions**, **seizure precautions**, **emergent labs**, or **rapid response activation**—rather than routine documentation alone.

**Exam fork:** If the stem clusters **peaked T waves**, **new confusion with Na 118**, or **Kussmaul breathing with gap acidosis**, prioritize **protocol-driven rescue and monitoring** over discharge planning.

sections.heading: Takeaways
sections.body:
- Tie **ECG + K⁺**, **neuro + Na⁺**, **lungs + weight + I&O**, and **ABG trend** before comfort-only tasks when risk is rising.
- Use the Fluids & Electrolytes topic hub (**`fluids-electrolytes`**) to cluster practice with related lessons.
- Link review: [Fluid balance acute care](LESSON:fluid-balance-acute-care) · [Na disorders](LESSON:hyponatremia-vs-hypernatremia-hy) · [K disorders](LESSON:hypo-vs-hyperkalemia-hy) · [ABG basics](LESSON:abg-interpretation-basics-hy) · [SIADH vs DI basics](LESSON:siadh-vs-di-basics) · [DKA vs HHS](LESSON:dka-vs-hhs-priorities-hy) · [pathway lesson hub](/us/rn/nclex-rn/lessons)

**Takeaway drill:** Name the **life threat**, the **two objective findings** you recheck first, and the **single escalation** that matches policy—then pick the answer that matches that sequence.

sections.heading: Exam relevance
sections.body:
Examiners use **Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis** to probe prioritization, monitoring, and escalation under time pressure.

Calculate or interpret **AG**—choose **source control + fluids + antibiotics** in sepsis, **insulin** in DKA, **fomepizole** themes in ethylene glycol when stem matches—avoid treating every gap acidosis with bicarbonate alone.

Eliminate answers that **skip ECG or neuro checks** when K or Na is extreme, **push IV potassium**, **correct sodium too fast** without monitoring, **ignore rising lactate**, or **delegate** unstable assessment. Boards often use "first," "priority," or "most important" language—choose the option that **reduces harm fastest** for the deteriorating client.

sections.heading: Core concept
sections.body:
**Nursing priorities (high yield)**
- **Delta-delta**: HCO₃ changes vs anion gap when triple disorder suspected.
- **K**: watch K shifts as acidosis corrects.
- **Renal failure**: uremia contributes to gap.
- **Nursing**: monitor I&O, perfusion, repeat lactate per sepsis bundles.

**Red flags / safety alerts**
- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.
- **Hyponatremia**: seizures or acute severe symptoms → **hypertonic saline** pathways with **strict correction limits**; **ODS** if corrected too fast.
- **Hypernatremia / DI**: **free water deficit** replacement **slowly**; track neuro status.
- **Fluid overload**: crackles + hypoxia + weight gain → **oxygen, positioning, provider activation**—not more isotonic bolus without indication.
- **Metabolic acidosis**: treat **cause** (sepsis, DKA, ischemic gut, toxins)—bicarbonate only when criteria match the stem.
- **COPD CO₂ narcosis**: **ventilation** strategy over blind high oxygen.

**Patient teaching (stable contexts)**
- Teach **daily weights**, **low-sodium diet literacy**, **fluid restriction measurement**, **when to call for swelling or SOB**, **oral rehydration** when appropriate, and **medication adherence** for diuretics/ACE inhibitors with lab monitoring.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.

- **Hyperkalemia**: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave—**calcium for ECG instability**, then **insulin/glucose** per orders; **never IV push** concentrated potassium.

**NCLEX-style clinical reasoning**
- Match **data → risk → first action**; avoid teaching-first answers when **rhythm, airway, seizure, or perfusion** is acutely threatened.
- When multiple clients compete, pick the client who **decompensates fastest** if unattended.
